title: Makki di Roti

overview.description: A traditional Punjabi flatbread made from maize flour, shaped by hand and cooked on a hot tawa until lightly browned and crisp at the edges.

method: Place the makki atta and salt in a large bowl.
Gradually add the hot water, mixing with a spoon until the flour is cool enough to handle.
Knead gently until a soft dough forms.
The dough should be moist and pliable but should not feel sticky.
Divide the dough into 6 equal portions.
Keep the portions covered while working to prevent them drying out.
Place one portion between two sheets of baking paper or plastic wrap.
Press and pat the dough into a thin circle approximately 15–18cm in diameter.
Carefully transfer the roti to a hot tawa or heavy frying pan.
Cook until the underside develops brown spots and the surface begins to dry.
Turn carefully and cook the other side.
Turn again if necessary until cooked through and lightly browned.
Brush with ghee or butter before serving.

learning.did-you-know: Makki di roti is a traditional Punjabi bread made from maize flour and is particularly famous as the classic accompaniment to sarson da saag.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How to work with a gluten-free dough and shape a flatbread by pressing and patting rather than rolling.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A distinctive Punjabi flatbread that introduces the children to maize flour and demonstrates how a bread can be made without relying on wheat gluten.
chapman-tips: Use hot water to help the maize flour hydrate and make the dough easier to handle.
Keep the dough covered because it dries out quickly.
Don't try to knead it like wheat dough; gentle mixing is enough.
Pat the dough out gradually rather than trying to make it thin immediately.
Baking paper makes transferring the fragile roti much easier.
If the dough cracks, gently press the edges back together with wet fingers.
The first one may be more of a learning experience than a perfect circle — that's completely normal.
notes.batch-cooking: Makki di roti is best fresh. If necessary, cooked rotis can be cooled and frozen, although they may become slightly more fragile after reheating.
notes.leftovers: Reheat gently on a dry tawa or frying pan and serve with butter, yogurt or leftover curry.
